metadata.summary: When extracting N entities from a document, never make the top-level structured-output response a bare array. Always wrap with items plus metadata like total_found and truncated.

content.title: Array Items Wrapper for Extraction
content.summary: When extracting N entities from a document, never make the top-level structured-output response a bare array. Always wrap with {items: [...]} plus metadata fields like total_found and truncated. Strict-mode validators reject top-level arrays because schema-level controls like additionalProperties are object-only; wrapping standardises the shape across zero/one/many cases and lets you add count and truncation diagnostics without reshaping consumers.

why.p: OpenAI strict mode and Azure structured outputs require the top-level schema to be an object — bare arrays are not allowed. Even on providers that permit it, asking the model for list[Entity] collapses three cases (zero, one, many) into shapes that downstream parsers handle inconsistently: zero entities sometimes returns [], sometimes null, sometimes a single dict; a single entity sometimes returns [ent], sometimes ent. Wrapping in {items: [...], total_found, truncated} removes the ambiguity, lets the model report "I found 0" or "I truncated at 50" explicitly, and keeps the consumer code single-shape. Simon Willison's --schema-multi pattern in llm formalises the same wrapper for batch extraction CLIs.

list.item: Any extraction with variable cardinality (entities, citations, line items, search hits).
Document-level summarisation that yields a list of facts/claims.
Batch classification where N inputs map to N outputs.
Any strict-mode SO call (OpenAI, Azure) — the top-level array is forbidden there anyway.
Hard-coded N (always exactly 3 suggestions) — use a fixed-length tuple type or named fields instead.
Single-entity extraction with no list semantics — wrap is overhead for a one-shot field.
Streaming UIs that render items as they arrive — items at top level can stream natively; wrap blocks streaming until the wrapper closes.

section.name: wrap-variable-cardinality-extraction
section.p: For any extraction with variable N, the top-level schema is an object containing items: list[Entity] plus total_found and truncated metadata. The bare list[Entity] return shape is forbidden because (a) strict mode requires top-level objects, (b) downstream code has to special-case zero/one/many.

rule.statement: For variable-cardinality extraction, declare the top-level schema as an object with at least items: list[Entity], total_found: int, and truncated: bool. Never return a bare list as the top-level structured-output type.
rule.rationale: OpenAI/Azure strict mode rejects top-level arrays because additionalProperties only applies to objects. On other providers, list[Entity] collapses zero/one/many into inconsistent shapes ([], null, single dict, [ent], ent). The wrapper standardises the shape and exposes hooks for count and truncation diagnostics.

example.title: Entity wrapper with metadata
example.code:
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field
class Entity(BaseModel): name: str kind: str span_start: int span_end: int
class EntityList(BaseModel): model_config = {"extra": "forbid"} total_found: int = Field(description="Count of entities; 0 if none.") truncated: bool = Field(description="True if more entities exist beyond the limit.") items: list[Entity]
example.note: One stable shape for zero, one, or many. total_found lets the consumer detect "no entities" without inspecting len(items); truncated flags incomplete extraction.

example.title: Bare array and optional list anti-patterns
example.code:
# Bare list — fails strict mode and ambiguous for zero/one cases. EntityList = list[Entity]
# Or worse: optional list with no metadata. class EntityListBad(BaseModel): items: list[Entity] | None = None
example.note: Strict-mode endpoints reject the top-level array. Optional-list shape forces every consumer to write if items is None or len(items) == 0.

antipattern.description: Asking for "extract all entities" and accepting the model's reply as either a dict (single entity) or a list (many entities).
antipattern.reason: The same prompt yields different top-level shapes depending on how many entities the model thinks it found. Production code ends up branching on isinstance(result, list) everywhere — a known source of crashes when an empty result returns null or {}.

template.purpose: Pydantic batch-entity-extraction schema with items plus total_found plus truncated

see-also.link: Simon Willison — llm --schema-multi pattern (the wrapper formalised in a CLI)
Databricks — end-to-end structured extraction with LLM, batch entity wrapper
